stem: A population has standard deviation \( \sigma = [[sigma]] \). For random samples of size \( n = [[n]] \), what is the standard error of the sample mean, \( \sigma/\sqrt{n} \)?
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( n \) instead of by \( \sqrt{n} \).
This multiplies by \( \sqrt{n} \) instead of dividing by it.
This is the population standard deviation itself; the standard error shrinks with sample size.
This is off by one from the correct standard error.

stem: A population has standard deviation \( \sigma = [[sigma]] \). What sample size \( n \) is required so that the standard error of the sample mean, \( \sigma/\sqrt{n} \), equals \( [[SE]] \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the ratio \( \sigma/[[SE]] \).
This squares only \( \sigma \) and forgets to also square \( [[SE]] \) in the denominator.
This inverts the ratio, dividing \( [[SE]] \) by \( \sigma \) instead of the other way around.
This squares only the denominator \( [[SE]] \), not the whole ratio.
This is off by one from the correct sample size.

stem: For random samples from a fixed population, the sample size is increased by a factor of \( [[fsq]] \) (that is, the new sample size is \( [[fsq]] \) times the old one). By what factor does the standard error of the sample mean change?
solution: The standard error is proportional to \( 1/\sqrt{n} \); multiplying \( n \) by \( [[fsq]] \) multiplies \( \sqrt{n} \) by \( [[f]] \), so the standard error is multiplied by \( 1/[[f]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the sample-size multiplier itself as the change factor for the standard error, rather than its square root.
This divides by the sample-size multiplier \( [[fsq]] \) directly, instead of by its square root \( [[f]] \).
This assumes the standard error grows by the same factor the sample size grows by.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct factor.

stem: For samples of size \( n_1 = [[n1]] \) drawn from a population, the standard error of the sample mean is \( [[SE1]] \). What would the standard error of the sample mean be for samples of size \( n_2 = [[n2]] \) drawn from the SAME population?
solution: Since \( \sigma = [[SE1]]\sqrt{n_1} \) stays fixed, the new standard error is \( [[SE1]]\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{n_1}}{\sqrt{n_2}} = [[SE1]]\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{[[n1]]}}{\sqrt{[[n2]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of sample sizes inside the square root.
This scales by the ratio of sample sizes directly, instead of by the ratio of their square roots.
This scales by the inverse ratio of sample sizes directly, instead of by the ratio of square roots.
This assumes the standard error is unaffected by the change in sample size.
This is off by one from the correct standard error.
